rationale: In the theory section, the citation is tied to a specific claim about decision difficulty (rejecting options is harder than adding), not just a generic background list. That claim is then operationalized directly in Study 1, where reject-versus-add framing is used to create the fluency discrepancy manipulation. Although cited alongside Park et al. (2000), this paper plays a clear functional role in justifying the experimental setup.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Novemsky and Kahneman (2005) as prior evidence that reject-framed option decisions are more difficult than add-framed decisions. This premise supports how the citing paper constructs expected-versus-actual difficulty discrepancies in its self-customization experiments.
